traditions: Water splashing; sand stupa building at temples; bathing Buddha images; baci ceremony; releasing birds and fish.
historicalBackground: Pi Mai Lao is the traditional Lao New Year, similar to Songkran but with unique Lao Buddhist ceremonies. It marks the beginning of the agricultural cycle.
description: Laos' gentle New Year celebration, where water rituals, temple visits, and the baci ceremony mark the renewal of the season.

name: Water Festival (Dagu)
traditions: Water throwing and dousing; decorated pandals with music; visiting pagodas; washing Buddha images; merit-making.
historicalBackground: Mandalay's Thingyan celebrations are among Myanmar's most traditional, centered around the Mahamyatmuni Pagoda and the ancient royal city.
description: Mandalay's traditional water festival celebrations, combining sacred Buddhist rituals with joyous water-dousing at the ancient royal city.

name: Songkran
traditions: Water splashing and water fights; visiting temples; building sand stupas; pouring scented water over elders' hands; releasing fish and birds.
historicalBackground: Songkran originated as a ritual cleansing for the Thai New Year based on the solar calendar. Water symbolizes purification and the washing away of misfortune.
description: The Thai New Year water festival, where streets transform into massive water fights symbolizing purification and renewal.

name: St. Patrick's Day
traditions: Parades; wearing green; shamrock displays; Irish music and dance; Greening landmarks (rivers, buildings); Guinness drinking.
historicalBackground: St. Patrick's Day honors Ireland's patron saint, who brought Christianity to Ireland in the 5th century. It became a global celebration through the Irish diaspora.
description: Ireland's global green celebration, honoring the patron saint with parades, music, and the worldwide greening of landmarks.

name: Sukkot (Jerusalem)
traditions: Building decorative sukkot across the city; priestly blessing at Western Wall; water libation ceremony; Simchat Beit HaShoeva celebrations.
historicalBackground: Jerusalem's Sukkot celebrations center on the Temple Mount traditions described in the Torah, including the unique priestly blessing with thousands attending.
description: The ancient harvest pilgrimage to Jerusalem, where the city fills with decorated sukkot and joyous celebrations at the Western Wall.

name: Sukkot
traditions: Building and dwelling in temporary shelters (sukkah); waving the Four Species (lulav and etrog); festive meals; Hoshanah Rabbah processions.
historicalBackground: Sukkot commemorates the 40 years of Israelite wandering in the desert, living in temporary shelters. It is also a harvest thanksgiving festival.
description: The Jewish Feast of Tabernacles, a week of dwelling in decorated outdoor huts and celebrating the autumn harvest.

name: Summer Solstice at Stonehenge
traditions: Watching sunrise over the Heel Stone; drumming and chanting; Druidic ceremonies; all-night celebrations; traditional music.
historicalBackground: Stonehenge was aligned to the summer solstice sunrise around 3000 BCE. Modern celebrations resumed in 1999 after access was restricted in 1985.
description: The magical dawn gathering at Stonehenge, where thousands watch the midsummer sunrise align with the ancient stones.

name: Tanabata (Star Festival)
traditions: Writing wishes on paper strips (tanzaku) and hanging them on bamboo; elaborate streamer decorations; parades; stargazing.
historicalBackground: Tanabata originated from the Chinese Qixi festival, celebrating the meeting of star-crossed lovers Orihime and Hikoboshi across the Milky Way.
description: Japan's romantic Star Festival, where cities are adorned with colorful streamers and wishes are hung on bamboo under the summer stars.

name: World Tango Day
traditions: Milongas (dance events); tango concerts; street performances; tango history exhibitions; Carlos Gardel tributes.
historicalBackground: World Tango Day falls on the birthday of Carlos Gardel, tango's most iconic singer, and coincides with tango composer Julio de Caro's birthday.
description: The global celebration of tango on Carlos Gardel's birthday, with milongas and performances honoring Argentina's most passionate art form.

name: Buenos Aires Tango Festival
traditions: Tango dance competitions (Mundial de Tango); milongas (dance events); outdoor performances; tango orchestra concerts; dance workshops.
historicalBackground: Tango originated in the working-class neighborhoods of Buenos Aires and Montevideo in the late 19th century, blending European, African, and indigenous influences.
description: The world's premier tango event, celebrating Argentina's iconic dance with competitions, milongas, and performances across Buenos Aires.

name: Winter Tango Festival (Tangomarkkinat)
traditions: Open-air tango dancing; tango singing competitions; crowning of Tango King and Queen; milongas in Seinäjoki.
historicalBackground: Finland adopted tango in the early 20th century, developing a uniquely melancholic Finnish style. The festival has been held annually since 1985.
description: Finland's beloved summer tango festival, where thousands dance the uniquely Finnish style of tango under northern summer skies.

name: Tết (Vietnamese New Year)
traditions: Family reunions; ancestral altar offerings; lucky money (lì xì) in red envelopes; kumquat and peach blossom displays; bánh chưng rice cakes.
historicalBackground: Tết Nguyên Đán is Vietnam's most important celebration, with roots in Chinese lunar New Year traditions adapted over millennia to Vietnamese culture.
description: Vietnam's most cherished holiday, a celebration of family, ancestors, and renewal during the Lunar New Year.

name: Thai Pongal (Sri Lanka)
traditions: Boiling rice in new clay pots until it overflows; decorating cattle; kolam designs; family gatherings; thanksgiving prayers.
historicalBackground: Thai Pongal in Sri Lanka follows the same Tamil harvest traditions as in India but with unique Sri Lankan customs and Buddhist influences.
description: Sri Lanka's Tamil harvest festival, where overflowing rice pots symbolize abundance and gratitude for the year's bounty.

name: Thaipusam (Singapore)
traditions: Kavadi processions from Sri Srinivasa Perumal Temple to Thendayuthapani Temple; milk pot carrying; body piercings; fire walking.
historicalBackground: Singapore's Thaipusam is one of the most spectacular outside India, with a 4.5 km procession through the city center drawing devotees from across Southeast Asia.
description: Singapore's dramatic Thaipusam procession, where devotees carry elaborate kavadi through city streets in a stunning display of faith.

name: Thaipusam
traditions: Kavadi (burden-bearing) rituals with body piercings; milk pot carrying; shaving heads; chariot processions; prayers to Lord Murugan.
historicalBackground: Thaipusam honors Lord Murugan receiving a divine spear (Vel) from Goddess Parvati to vanquish evil. The festival emphasizes penance and devotion.
description: A Hindu Tamil festival of devotion to Lord Murugan, famous for its dramatic kavadi rituals and processions at Batu Caves, Malaysia.

name: Thanksgiving
traditions: Turkey dinner with stuffing and cranberry sauce; pumpkin pie; Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade; football games; family gatherings.
historicalBackground: Thanksgiving commemorates the 1621 harvest feast shared by Plymouth colonists and Wampanoag people. It became a national holiday in 1863 under Abraham Lincoln.
description: America's beloved harvest celebration, bringing families together for turkey dinners, parades, and gratitude on the fourth Thursday of November.

name: Thingyan (Water Festival)
traditions: Water dousing and water fights; mandats (water-throwing stages); religious observances; washing Buddha images; hair washing of elders.
historicalBackground: Thingyan marks the Myanmar New Year and has Buddhist origins. Water symbolizes the cleansing of sins from the old year.
description: Myanmar's exuberant water festival celebrating the Buddhist New Year, where cities transform into massive water-soaked celebrations.

name: Tihar (Festival of Lights)
traditions: Five days honoring crows, dogs, cows, oxen, and siblings; oil lamps and candles; Deusi-Bhailo singing; rangoli; card games.
historicalBackground: Tihar is Nepal's version of Diwali but includes unique days for honoring animals. Kukur Tihar (Dog Day) has gained global attention for garlanding dogs.
description: Nepal's unique five-day Festival of Lights, where crows, dogs, cows, and siblings are each honored on successive days.

name: Timkat
traditions: Processions carrying replica Arks of the Covenant (tabots); blessing of water; mass baptism; white clothing; three-day celebration with feasting and music.
historicalBackground: Timkat celebrates the Epiphany — the baptism of Jesus in the Jordan River. Ethiopia's unique Orthodox tradition dates to the 4th century Aksumite Kingdom.
description: Ethiopian Orthodox Epiphany celebration with dramatic processions, water blessings, and one of Africa's most colorful religious festivals.

name: Tsechu
traditions: Masked cham dances; unfurling giant thongdrel (silk appliqué); traditional music; archery; community feasting.
historicalBackground: Tsechus are annual Buddhist festivals held in every district of Bhutan, dating to the 17th century under Zhabdrung Ngawang Namgyal.
description: Bhutan's spectacular masked dance festivals, where monks perform sacred cham dances in colorful monasteries across the Himalayan kingdom.

name: Tulip Festival (Keukenhof)
traditions: Viewing 7 million flower bulbs; themed gardens; flower parades; art installations; tulip workshops.
historicalBackground: Keukenhof was established in 1949 as a flower exhibition. Tulips were introduced to the Netherlands from Turkey in the 16th century, sparking 'Tulip Mania.'
description: The world's largest flower garden, showcasing 7 million tulips and spring blooms in a dazzling display of Dutch horticultural artistry.

name: Up Helly Aa
traditions: Torchlit procession of guizers in Viking costumes; burning of a replica Viking longship; squad performances; all-night celebrations.
historicalBackground: Up Helly Aa began in the 1880s, replacing earlier rowdy celebrations. It honors Shetland's Norse heritage and the Viking influence on the islands.
description: Shetland's spectacular fire festival honoring Norse heritage with a dramatic torchlit procession and the burning of a Viking longship.

name: Vaisakhi
traditions: Visiting gurdwaras; Nagar Kirtan processions; performing bhangra dance; langar communal meals; reading of scriptures; fairs and markets.
historicalBackground: Vaisakhi commemorates the founding of the Khalsa by Guru Gobind Singh in 1699. It also marks the Punjabi New Year and spring harvest festival.
description: The Sikh New Year and harvest festival commemorating the founding of the Khalsa, celebrated with processions, music, and communal feasts.

name: Venice Biennale
traditions: National pavilions in Giardini; Arsenale exhibitions; collateral events; Golden Lion awards; art installations across Venice.
historicalBackground: The Venice Biennale was founded in 1895 as one of the first international art exhibitions. It has expanded to include architecture, cinema, dance, music, and theater.
description: The world's most prestigious international art exhibition, showcasing contemporary art from over 80 countries across Venice's historic venues.

name: Regata Storica
traditions: Historical boat procession on Grand Canal; gondola and multi-oar boat races; 16th-century costumes; Bucintoro replica.
historicalBackground: The Regata Storica dates to 1315 and is Venice's most important traditional event, celebrating the city's maritime heritage and gondolier skill.
description: Venice's spectacular historic regatta, a pageant of Renaissance-costumed boats and fierce gondola races on the Grand Canal.

name: Vesak
traditions: Visiting temples; meditation and prayer; bathing Buddha statues; releasing caged birds; lantern processions; acts of generosity to monks and the poor.
historicalBackground: Vesak celebrates the birth, enlightenment, and death of Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha). It is the most sacred day in the Buddhist calendar, observed since ancient times.
description: The most important Buddhist festival, celebrating the birth, enlightenment, and passing of the Buddha on the full moon day of May.

name: Victory Day
traditions: Military parade on Red Square; Immortal Regiment march carrying portraits of WWII veterans; laying wreaths; concerts; fireworks.
historicalBackground: Victory Day celebrates the Soviet Union's defeat of Nazi Germany on May 9, 1945. It is one of Russia's most important holidays.
description: Russia's solemn and triumphant commemoration of victory in WWII, centered on the grand military parade through Red Square.

name: Vivid Sydney
traditions: Light projections on Sydney Opera House and buildings; light sculptures; music performances; creative industry talks.
historicalBackground: Vivid Sydney began in 2009 as a creative industries event. It has grown into the world's largest festival of light, music, and ideas.
description: The world's largest festival of light, transforming Sydney's harbor and Opera House into a dazzling canvas of projected art.

name: Waitangi Day
traditions: Treaty commemorations; Māori cultural performances; waka (war canoe) ceremonies; hāngi (earth oven feast); official speeches.
historicalBackground: Waitangi Day marks the signing of the Treaty of Waitangi in 1840 between Māori chiefs and the British Crown, establishing New Zealand.
description: New Zealand's national day commemorating the founding treaty between Māori and the Crown, celebrated with cultural performances and reflection.

name: Walpurgis Night
traditions: Bonfires; dancing around maypoles; witch costumes; traditional songs; celebrating the arrival of spring; parades.
historicalBackground: Walpurgis Night blends pre-Christian spring celebrations with the feast of Saint Walpurga. In Germanic folklore, witches gather on the Brocken mountain.
description: A night of bonfires and revelry across Northern Europe, celebrating spring's arrival with ancient traditions of fire, dance, and witch folklore.

name: White Nights Festival
traditions: Ballet and opera at the Mariinsky Theatre; Scarlet Sails celebration; drawbridge openings; midnight sun walks; classical music concerts.
historicalBackground: The festival celebrates St. Petersburg's unique geography at 59°N, where the sun barely sets in June. Peter the Great founded the city to embrace this phenomenon.
description: St. Petersburg's magical summer celebration during the midnight sun, featuring world-class ballet, opera, and the iconic Scarlet Sails spectacle.

name: Dongzhi Festival
traditions: Eating tangyuan (glutinous rice balls) in southern China; jiaozi (dumplings) in northern China; family reunions; ancestral worship.
historicalBackground: Dongzhi has been celebrated since the Han Dynasty as one of the most important solar terms. It marks the return of longer daylight hours.
description: China's Winter Solstice festival, where families gather to eat warming tangyuan and dumplings, celebrating the return of longer days.

name: New Yam Festival (Iri Ji)
traditions: Offering the first yams to ancestors and deities; communal feasting; masquerade dances; wrestling matches; palm wine drinking.
historicalBackground: The New Yam Festival (Iri Ji Ohuru) predates colonialism and marks the end of a farming cycle. Yam is the king of crops in Igbo culture.
description: The Igbo harvest thanksgiving, celebrating the sacred first yam of the season with ancestral offerings, masquerades, and communal feasts.

name: Yom Kippur
traditions: 25-hour fast; intensive synagogue prayers; Kol Nidre evening service; wearing white; abstaining from work, leather shoes, and bathing.
historicalBackground: Yom Kippur is the holiest day in Judaism, established in the Torah. It is the Day of Atonement when Jews seek forgiveness from God for sins of the past year.
description: The Day of Atonement, the holiest day in the Jewish calendar dedicated to fasting, prayer, and spiritual reflection.

name: Yule (Jól)
traditions: Yule Lads (Jólasveinar) visiting children for 13 nights; Yule Cat legend; hangikjöt (smoked lamb); laufabrauð (leaf bread).
historicalBackground: Icelandic Yule traditions preserve Viking-era midwinter celebrations. The 13 Yule Lads descend from the mountains, one per night before Christmas.
description: Iceland's atmospheric midwinter celebration, where ancient Viking traditions blend with the 13 mischievous Yule Lads visiting over Christmas.
